title: 'The Boys' Season 5 Never Ended – You Just Need To Know Where To Look

blocks.content: Warning: Spoilers ahead for The Boys Season 5 finale!The Boys Season 5's finale, "Blood and Bone," made waves in the Amazon show's fandom for being something of a divisive way to end the story. Personally, I didn't have a problem with it. I can understand others' desire to see Antony Starr's Homelander cause a little more violent havoc before Butcher (Karl Urban) takes him down, but I don't think it was necessary. The Boys was rarely about mass-scale debauchery. As such, it would have been disingenuous to resort to that in the finale.

Either way, for those who wanted more after "Blood and Bone," The Boys has granted their wish. Since the show's final episode, the story has continued in an incredibly creative way. While there aren't any additional episodes of the VCU's flagship project, the franchise has still continued to flesh out the canon since The Boys ended. If other properties have handled their post-finale fallout in this way, I've never seen it. I think it's great, and I sort of hope it never stops.
Vought International's Social Media Feeds Canonically Continue 'The Boys' Season 5

The Boys has never been traditional in how it handles its episode synopses and other marketing information. Instead, almost everything is worded like an intricately constructed press release. Sometimes, the content of the episode itself is largely ignored by what's written. One of my favorite examples is the supposed synopsis of The Boys Season 5, Episode 6, "Though the Heavens Fall." It reads: "Available now, EXCLUSIVELY at @VMCtheaters, get the new #DeepPopcornBucket for only $32.99!" This meta, self-aware tactic of promoting The Boys isn't unique to Prime Video, where the show is hosted.

It can also be witnessed across various social media feeds. Although The Boys, as a show, does have traditional social media pages, the most entertaining way to engage with the franchise on platforms like X and Instagram is by following the official Vought International account. Amazon runs the Vought feeds in a very meta way. Everything is written as though it's being posted in-universe, and often in reaction to events that have happened within the world of The Boys and its spin-offs. Since "Blood and Bone" aired, Vought has continued to post about what transpired in the Season 5 finale, including clever reactions to the fates of various characters.

One of the more recent Vought posts acknowledges the fates of various characters who made their respective exits in "Blood and Bone," although most reports have been worded to paint Vought in the best possible light. Granted, most of the worldbuilding on these feeds, while really creative, doesn't add all that much to the overall canon. Still, I really like that so much effort continues to go into maintaining the illusion of Vought International being a real organization. Posts like the ones about the trigger warning that has been included on fictitious streaming service Vought+ ahead of movies like Dawn of the Seven that "present a traitor like Homelander in a positive light," are just an absolute chef's kiss of marketing from The Boys.
